Transcrição Completa: The Profit First Business Model

 

[00:00] [music] I read this incredible book. It was called Profit First. I can't pronounce this guy's last name, but his first name is Mike. If you want to go buy the book and check it out yourself.

 

[00:11] The concept of it is quite simple. Sales minus profit equals expenses.

 

[00:18] This changed my whole concept on how I was operating my business.

 

[00:22] I knew this guy's name was Bill Walsh. He runs this uh he runs a gala charity called U4G. Uh he also has a big business, one of the biggest uh conferences and conventions across the United States here called the Small Biz Expo.

 

[00:37] You know, thousands of people show up per event depending on where it's located.

 

[00:42] And Bill, you know, he's he's uh behind the scenes at this event that he's running for for his gala, which is a big uh kind of charity event where everybody dresses up all fancy and it was in Beverly Hills off Rodeo Drive. Real fancy place.

 

[00:57] Long story short, tickets were like 5K a pop.

 

[01:01] Bill invites me in because I was one of the people that was speaking on his small biz expo tours just in a few different cities where I would be at at the same time. So, we had a good relationship together.

 

[01:13] Now, long story short, I asked Bill as a mentor because he's much older than me and I always ask people mentor related questions when I can tell they're in places I want to be at a future point, right?

 

[01:24] So, I tell Bill I I explain to him I say, "Bill, you know, at the time I'm making $80,000 a month, uh, but my expenses are through the roof. I'm spending like $60,000 a month just to be in business.

 

[01:39] You know, I got rent and all these offices and my payroll, it's always this and I'm spending money on ads, too. And like this is how much I'm spending on ads and then my taxes. I mean, after you take out taxes alone, just being here in California, my god.

 

[01:53] I said, you know, I I'm only netting $20,000 a month, but I'm making 80,000. What do you What do you think of that?

 

[02:01] He says, you know, remember this, Jeremy. Why would you only why would you work to make 80,000? Meaning the effort that's required to make 80,000 and only net 20,000 that's simply not enough money.

 

[02:17] He says you have to be able to handle the expenses and reduce them at the time.

 

[02:22] That was very high impact for me. It helped me understand that I was spending too much, but I didn't have a practical way to lower my expenses and I didn't really know what to do.

 

[02:33] So anyway, I got I got led to this book called Profit First. And here here this book changes my whole perspective on business again for the better.

 

[02:42] Like I said, the core concept, whatever your desired profit is, that should be the amount you expense first. Period.

 

[02:51] So, for example, if I sell a $10,000 package and I want $7,500 worth of profit, that means it's $10,000 minus $7,500 equals 2500 left for me to pay my expenses, which completely changed how I ran my business.

 

[03:09] because then the desired profit that I would want every deal would happen no matter what because I thought with profit first.

 

[03:16] Then I would have whatever amount of money left over to expense on things like payroll, contractors, uh really anything.

 

[03:24] And I want to be clear, this is also an automated process for when the money hits your account.

 

[03:29] So as an example, I have one main bank account that handles the operating cost of my agency.

 

[03:35] From there, you can go into a bank and do this at your local branch, depending on who you bank with, and you can create multiple accounts that you can automatically siphon a percentage off into for different examples.

 

[03:48] So, on Profit First, Mike talks about having one for your operating account, having one for taxes, having one for personal, having one for your expenses for XYZ that's specific to you, having one for your asset or your emergency fund.

 

[04:04] And then long story short, anytime money comes into your business, it automatically after going into that one main account gets divided from whatever percentage you set up to automatically go into all these other accounts from the gross amount that was taken out.

 

[04:20] So I like to say with the profit first, it's sales minus your taxes for wherever you're at, then minus your profit equals the expenses that you have left because you never want to ignore tax.

 

[04:33] So long story short, guys, make sure that you really consider this for whatever you're selling. This really helps with pricing your package.

 

[04:41] So, as an example, if you're thinking, "Oh, I'm going to sell something for $2,000, but you want to make $50,000 a month." It can be a really clear way for you to see how bad you're going to hurt yourself if you would take that approach because the expenses you have, the profit that you desire, it just wouldn't add up if that initial number of how much you're making per sale doesn't add up to make the rest of it make sense.

 

[05:08] So, anyway, automate the process by going to a bank, having several bank accounts. some of the key bank accounts that I would recommend opening an operating account which is where you put all your expenses for the business taxes account you never touch just for taxes don't even have a debit card or a credit card credit card linked to that and preferably open your tax account in a different bank that way it's at a different banking location and c and force you to wire money out of that account if you would ever have a need to wire money out of that account have an emergency fund wire money to yourself personally as well in this method and then in addition to that whatever other accounts might be applicable to you including a savings things, a wealth buildinging fund, could be anything.

 

[05:55] Uh, from there, that's where you have your expenses. So, whatever's left inside of the operating account, that's what you you then dish out and and more importantly are able to think with. Okay?

 

[06:06] So, the idea of closing a $10,000 deal and just having $10,000 is no longer a thing. It becomes, okay, out of a $10,000 deal, I only have $1,800 for my expenses.

 

[06:18] What is possible for me to do with that money to get the best result for this client?

 

[06:23] Rather than the thinking of, okay, I got $10,000. What's the best thing for me to do with this client to get them a result? Might be more expensive in that approach.

 

[06:32] So, it changes your mentality as well as saves you a lot of money automatically.

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:

Faturamento mensal do exemplo: $80,000/mês

Despesas mensais do exemplo: $60,000/mês

Lucro líquido real: $20,000/mês (25% de margem)

Exemplo de pacote: $10,000 de venda

Lucro desejado no exemplo: $7,500 (75%)

Despesas disponíveis: $2,500 (25%)

Exemplo de produto low-ticket: $2,000

Meta mensal desejada: $50,000/mês

Ingresso do evento gala: $5,000

Exemplo final: $10,000 de venda = apenas $1,800 para despesas

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:

 

Fórmula Profit First:

Modelo tradicional (errado): Vendas - Despesas = Lucro

Modelo Profit First (correto): Vendas - Lucro - Impostos = Despesas

 

Sistema de Múltiplas Contas Bancárias:

Conta Operacional (Operating Account) - todas as despesas do negócio

Conta de Impostos (Taxes Account) - nunca tocar, sem cartões vinculados, preferencialmente em banco diferente

Conta Pessoal (Personal) - transferências para você

Conta de Emergência (Emergency Fund) - reserva de segurança

Contas específicas adicionais - poupança, construção de patrimônio, etc.

 

Automação Financeira:

Dinheiro entra na conta principal

Divisão automática por percentuais pré-definidos

Distribuição imediata para todas as contas secundárias

Sistema impede gastos excessivos automaticamente

 

ESTRUTURA:

 

Mentoria recebida:

Bill Walsh (mentor)

Organizador da U4G Gala Charity

Criador do Small Biz Expo (milhares de participantes por evento)

Evento em Beverly Hills, Rodeo Drive

 

Livro referência:

"Profit First" - Autor: Mike (sobrenome não pronunciado)

Conceito central: Lucro primeiro, despesas depois

 

Mudança de mentalidade:

De: "Tenho $10,000, o que posso fazer?"

Para: "Tenho $1,800 para despesas, como otimizo?"

 

Benefícios do sistema:

Lucro garantido em cada venda

Controle automático de despesas

Impostos sempre separados

Emergências cobertas

Precificação mais estratégica

Prevenção de gastos excessivos

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS:

 

Caso real apresentado:

Receita mensal: $80,000

Despesas operacionais: $60,000

Lucro líquido: $20,000

Margem de lucro real: 25%

Problema identificado: Esforço para $80k resultando em apenas $20k líquidos

 

Exemplo prático do modelo:

Venda: $10,000

Lucro desejado: $7,500 (75%)

Despesas disponíveis: $2,500 (25%)

Nova mentalidade: trabalhar com apenas $1,800 para despesas operacionais

 

Impacto da precificação:

Produto de $2,000 com meta de $50,000/mês

Sistema revela inviabilidade do modelo

Força reconsideração de preços e estrutura

 

Estrutura de custos Califórnia:

Impostos estaduais mencionados como significativos

Rent de múltiplos escritórios

Folha de pagamento

Investimento em anúncios

 

DURAÇÃO: ~6:40 minutos FORMATO: Vídeo educacional narrativo (storytelling + ensino prático)
